---
title: "The double slit experiment, part 1: the path integral approach and getting through the slits"
description: >
  Deriving the free-particle propagator from the path integral, propagating a
  Gaussian electron beam to a two-slit screen, and computing the probability of
  passing through either slit three independent ways.
date: 2026-07-10
author: "Kyle Arean-Raines"
categories: [quantum mechanics, path integrals, python]
image: thumbnail.png
toc: true
bibliography: ../../references.bib
---

In this series we'll be using numerical and analytical methods for simulating
and solving for the result of the famous quantum mechanical [double-slit experiment](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Double-slit_experiment),
performed with an electron and solved in a variety of different ways. As this is an advanced topic,
having some familiarity with calculus and perhaps basic classical mechanics will help a great deal.
That said, the accompanying derivations and code can be skipped if need be, and some calculations are omitted and left 
as an exercise for the reader in any case (cue sinister laugh).

I like this problem a lot, as it illustrates some really interesting quantum phenomena 
(superposition, interference, uncertainty, measurement, decoherence), and you can layer on the complexity, 
as we'll do here in discrete and progressive steps.

Though two of three grad courses I took in quantum mechanics referenced this experiment, the treatment was mostly 
qualitative (even though more rigorous mathematical machinery was subsequently developed throughout the course). Further,
we didn't do much until Quantum II involving path integrals, which is an alternative way of formulating it that
typically isn't taught in introductory classes on the subject. But this double-slit problem is actually relatively
tractable using this alternative framework. I was inspired also by the book by Richard Feynman and Albert Hibbs called 
_Quantum Mechanics and Path Integrals_. One of the first problems they look at is single-slit diffraction, and the 
intuition for the path integral is built up using one, then multiple, slits in a screen (a continuum of screens in the limit).

In Part 1 we will introduce this technique, then we'll work through the derivations in multiple posts.
The starting point will be the experimental setup along with a quick primer on the classical Lagrangian and action, followed
by a quick overview of path integrals in physics. Then we'll derive the propagator and probability amplitude
for the free electron in our experiment and take it from there.

Everything in this series is generated from a single physics module, `dslit.py`,
that lives in the repository. Each figure below is produced when the page is
rendered, so the plots can never drift from the equations that describe them.

## The setup

A free electron leaves the origin traveling in $+x$
toward a screen a distance $L_1$ away. The screen has two slits: the top slit
spans $y \in [+a/2,\, +a/2+b]$ and the bottom slit $y \in [-a/2-b,\, -a/2]$, so
the central barrier between them has height $a$ and each opening has height $b$.

```{python}
#| label: fig-schematic
#| fig-cap: "Geometry. A Gaussian beam leaves the source and drifts a distance $L_1$ to a screen with two slits of height $b$ separated by $a$."
#| code-fold: true
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import dslit

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 4.2))
dslit.draw_schematic(ax)
plt.show()
```

## The free-particle kernel

The propagator is the sum over all paths of $e^{iS/\hbar}$. For the free particle
Lagrangian $\mathcal{L} = \tfrac12 m\dot{x}^2$, slice the time interval $T$ into
$N$ steps of width $\varepsilon = T/N$:

$$
K = \lim_{N\to\infty}\left(\frac{m}{2\pi i\hbar\varepsilon}\right)^{N/2}
\int \prod_{j=1}^{N-1} dx_j\;
\exp\!\left[\frac{i}{\hbar}\sum_{j=0}^{N-1}\frac{m(x_{j+1}-x_j)^2}{2\varepsilon}\right].
$$ {#eq-slices}

Each intermediate integral is Gaussian, and the convolution of two free kernels
of durations $\varepsilon_1,\varepsilon_2$ is again a free kernel of duration
$\varepsilon_1+\varepsilon_2$. The product in @eq-slices therefore collapses to a
single Gaussian:

$$
\boxed{\;K(x_b,T;x_a,0)=\sqrt{\frac{m}{2\pi i\hbar T}}\;
\exp\!\left[\frac{i m (x_b-x_a)^2}{2\hbar T}\right]\;}
$$ {#eq-kernel}

In two dimensions the action separates, so $K(\mathbf{r}_b,T;\mathbf{r}_a,0)=K_x\,K_y$
with the same form in each coordinate. The $x$-motion is free drift that fixes the
arrival time $T_1 = L_1/v$; all of the interference lives in the transverse
coordinate $y$, so from here on $K_y$ is the object we push through the slits.

## A Gaussian beam, and where it is by the time it reaches the slits

"Traveling in $+x$" means a narrow spread of transverse momentum, i.e. a Gaussian
transverse profile of waist $\sigma_0$ at the source. Convolving that Gaussian
with @eq-kernel keeps it Gaussian, and its width at the slit plane is

$$
\sigma_{T_1}^2 = \sigma_0^2 + \left(\frac{\hbar T_1}{2 m \sigma_0}\right)^2 .
$$ {#eq-spread}

With a 1 keV electron, a 10 cm drift, and a 1 µm waist:

```{python}
#| label: derived
#| code-fold: true
d = dslit.derived_quantities()
print(f"de Broglie wavelength : {d['lam']*1e12:6.2f} pm")
print(f"speed                 : {d['v']:.3e} m/s  ({d['v']/2.998e8:.3f} c)")
print(f"flight time T1        : {d['T1']*1e9:6.3f} ns")
print(f"beam width at slits   : {d['sigmaT']*1e6:6.3f} um")
```

## Probability of passing through either slit

The transmission is the modulus squared $|\psi(y,T_1)|^2$, integrated over the two openings. Because
the arriving beam is a Gaussian of width $\sigma_{T_1}$ and the two slits are
mirror images, this collapses to a difference of error functions:

$$
P_{\text{either}} = \operatorname{erf}\!\left(\frac{a/2+b}{\sqrt{2}\,\sigma_{T_1}}\right)
- \operatorname{erf}\!\left(\frac{a/2}{\sqrt{2}\,\sigma_{T_1}}\right).
$$ {#eq-transmission}

Three independent computations agree: the closed form @eq-transmission, direct
quadrature of $|\psi|^2$ over the openings, and a full Fresnel convolution of the
Gaussian against the kernel @eq-kernel (which also validates the kernel itself,
since it reproduces the analytic spreading of @eq-spread).

```{python}
#| label: transmission
#| code-fold: true
import numpy as np

d = dslit.derived_quantities()
P_cf = dslit.P_closed_form(sigmaT=d["sigmaT"])
P_q = dslit.P_quadrature(sigmaT=d["sigmaT"])

# kernel-propagation check against the analytic spreading Gaussian
y = np.linspace(-4e-6, 4e-6, 41)
dens_num = np.abs(dslit.propagate_via_kernel(y, d["T1"]))**2
dens_ana = np.exp(-y**2/(2*d["sigmaT"]**2)) / (np.sqrt(2*np.pi)*d["sigmaT"])
rel = np.max(np.abs(dens_num - dens_ana)) / np.max(dens_ana)

print(f"P (closed form erf)        = {P_cf:.6f}")
print(f"P (quadrature)             = {P_q:.6f}")
print(f"|difference|               = {abs(P_cf-P_q):.1e}")
print(f"kernel vs analytic |psi|^2 = {rel:.1e} (max relative error)")
```

About 28% of the beam makes it through; the rest is stopped by the central
septum and the outer walls. The figure below shows the intensity arriving at the
slit plane with the two openings shaded, overlaid with the kernel-propagated
points to show the derivation and the numerics land on the same curve.

```{python}
#| label: fig-slitplane
#| fig-cap: "Intensity at the slit plane. Shaded bands are the two openings; the shaded area is $P_{\\text{either}}$. Points are the Fresnel-propagated kernel, the curve is the analytic Gaussian."
#| code-fold: true
BLUE, AMBER = "#185FA5", "#BA7517"
sigmaT = d["sigmaT"]
yy = np.linspace(-4e-6, 4e-6, 800)
dens = np.exp(-yy**2/(2*sigmaT**2)) / (np.sqrt(2*np.pi)*sigmaT)
yk = np.linspace(-4e-6, 4e-6, 61)
densk = np.abs(dslit.propagate_via_kernel(yk, d["T1"]))**2

a, b = dslit.DEFAULTS["a"], dslit.DEFAULTS["b"]
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 4.2))
ax.plot(yy*1e6, dens/1e6, color=BLUE, lw=2, label=r"analytic $|\psi(y,T_1)|^2$")
ax.plot(yk*1e6, densk/1e6, "o", ms=4, color=AMBER, mfc="none",
        label="kernel (Fresnel) propagation")
for lo, hi in [(a/2, a/2+b), (-a/2-b, -a/2)]:
    ax.axvspan(lo*1e6, hi*1e6, color=BLUE, alpha=0.15)
ax.set_xlabel(r"$y$ at slit plane [$\mu$m]")
ax.set_ylabel(r"probability density [$\mu$m$^{-1}$]")
ax.legend(frameon=False)
plt.show()
```

## How transmission depends on the knobs

Sweeping the geometry and the beam shows two effects worth noting. A faster
electron spends less time in flight, spreads less by @eq-spread, and is therefore
blocked *more* by the central septum, so $P$ falls with energy for this on-axis
layout. And there is a focusing optimum in the waist: too tight a $\sigma_0$
spreads quickly by the uncertainty term, too loose is already wide at the source.
The minimum-spread waist is $\sigma_0=\sqrt{\hbar T_1/m}$.

```{python}
#| label: fig-sweeps
#| fig-cap: "Transmission versus slit height, electron energy, and beam waist. Dashed lines mark the defaults; the dotted line is the minimum-spread waist."
#| code-fold: true
GRAY = "#5F5E5A"
E0, s0 = dslit.DEFAULTS["E_eV"], dslit.DEFAULTS["sigma0"]
fig, ax = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(11, 3.4))

bb = np.linspace(0.05, 4, 200)*1e-6
ax[0].plot(bb*1e6, [dslit.P_closed_form(a=a, b=bi) for bi in bb], color=BLUE, lw=2)
ax[0].axvline(b*1e6, color=GRAY, ls="--", lw=1)
ax[0].set_xlabel(r"slit height $b$ [$\mu$m]"); ax[0].set_ylabel("P(either slit)")

EE = np.logspace(1.5, 4.5, 200)
PE = [dslit.P_closed_form(sigmaT=dslit.derived_quantities(E_eV=Ei)["sigmaT"]) for Ei in EE]
ax[1].semilogx(EE, PE, color=BLUE, lw=2); ax[1].axvline(E0, color=GRAY, ls="--", lw=1)
ax[1].set_xlabel("electron energy [eV]")

ss = np.logspace(-7.3, -5.3, 200)
PS = [dslit.P_closed_form(sigmaT=dslit.derived_quantities(sigma0=si)["sigmaT"]) for si in ss]
sopt = np.sqrt(dslit.hbar*d["T1"]/dslit.m_e)
ax[2].semilogx(ss*1e6, PS, color=BLUE, lw=2)
ax[2].axvline(s0*1e6, color=GRAY, ls="--", lw=1)
ax[2].axvline(sopt*1e6, color=AMBER, ls=":", lw=1.5)
ax[2].set_xlabel(r"beam waist $\sigma_0$ [$\mu$m]")
fig.tight_layout(); plt.show()
```

## Next

We now have the kernel, a beam that reaches the slits, and the fraction that gets
through. [Part 2](../double-slit-part2/) propagates the clipped wavefunction to
a detection screen and builds the interference pattern two
ways: an analytic closed form for Gaussian slits and a numeric Fresnel integral
for hard slits. [Part 3](../double-slit-part3/) couples the slits to a which-path
detector and watches the fringes wash out.

The full source for this series is on
[GitHub](https://github.com/kareanra/physics-blog).
